Press enter or click to view image in full size

For years, ESLint was just part of my workflow.
I saved a file, waited a few seconds, fixed the errors, and moved on.
At first, those few seconds didn’t seem like a problem. But after hundreds of saves every day, the delay became impossible to ignore. The linter had quietly become one of the slowest parts of my development experience.
That’s when I started asking a simple question:
Is ESLint still the best option for a modern React + TypeScript project in 2026?
I thought the answer would take one evening to find.
It took almost an entire week.
The Problem Nobody Notices
Most developers don’t replace ESLint because it’s broken. They replace it because the friction gradually adds up.
In my case, the biggest issue was feedback speed. Every time I changed a file, I had to wait before ESLint finished analyzing the code and reported potential issues. Running the linter across the entire project was even more noticeable.
So I decided to measure it instead of relying on intuition.
The benchmark command was simple:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

benchmarksSeeing the numbers side by side was the moment I realized this wasn’t just a minor optimization.
The difference was dramatic.
The Rabbit Hole
Once I started researching alternatives, I quickly discovered that the JavaScript linting ecosystem had changed a lot.
I looked at:

• ESLint

• Oxlint

• Biome

• XO

• StandardJS

• Several older linting tools
After a few days of testing, benchmarking, and reading documentation, only two serious candidates remained:
Oxlint
Fast • ESLint-compatible
Biome
Fast • All-in-one toolchain
At that point, I was almost certain I would choose Biome.
It was incredibly fast, included a formatter, organized imports automatically, and promised an all-in-one developer experience.
On paper, it looked like the obvious winner.
Then I tried migrating my existing ESLint configuration.
What I Didn’t Expect
The hardest part of the migration wasn’t performance.
It wasn’t installation.
It wasn’t even the configuration format.
It was compatibility.
I had accumulated a carefully tuned set of ESLint rules over time, especially for React and TypeScript. Reproducing the same behavior in a new tool turned out to be much more complicated than simply running a migration command.
That was the moment the evaluation changed from:
“Which linter is faster?”
to:
“Which linter can actually replace my existing setup without changing the team’s coding standards?”
In the next section
I’ll show why I originally planned to adopt Biome, what happened when I started migrating real rules, and the specific challenge that ultimately changed my decision.
And surprisingly, that challenge had nothing to do with linting performance.
Estimated reading time: ~3–4 minutes
